Merck has significant depreciation and amortization expenses that increase its FCF relative to earnings. The more FCF that is generated, the greater the flexibility a company has to invest in R&D (research and development) or to acquire smaller companies with appealing technology that has the potential to generate future growth. A Price to FCF ratio of less than 15 is excellent.

Compared to the average of it peer group, Merck appears to be a quality company in an industry which has historically provided investors with superior returns. I believe that the recent negative perception of the pharmaceutical industry provides an opportunity for long-term investors and that Merck, in particular, has positioned itself for a robust future.

Valuation

Now I will value Merck’s stock using two different methods. First, I want to use the DDM (dividend discount model) using the 5% dividend growth rate. My DDM formula looks like this:

(D/(M-X)) = V

Where:

V = Fair Value

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount rate (I use my required rate of return on investment)

X = Expected future average annual increase in dividend

(3.24/(.09-.65)=129.60

I expect mature company stocks like Merck to provide a total return of about 9%, the current dividend is $3.24 and the expected growth in the dividend is 6.5%. When we plug these numbers into the model the result is $129.60 per share, well above the current share price of about $79.89. This indicates that the price is undervalued, selling at a discount to fair value of 38%. I believe that the price will achieve it fair value within the next five years so my expected annual compound average gain will be 10.2% for the valuation increase. The Excel formula is:

=((129.60/79.89)^(1/5))-1

If we add the 10.2% annual valuation gain to the dividend yield of 4% and the expected EPS growth rate of 13%, we end up with an expected compound annual average growth rate of 27.2%. It would be difficult to do much better without accepting greater risk.

Now I want to look at the value from a multiple perspective, assuming that the PE ratio will revert to its historical mean over the next five years. The 10-year average PE for Merck is 13. The current PE is 9, so by this method, the stock is trading at only 69% of fair value. From this assessment we find that fair value is $116.09. So, if we calculate the compound average annual increase from a multiple expansion, we get 7.6%. The calculation in Excel is as follows:

=((13/9)^(1/5))-1

Then we add to the annual expected valuation increase of 7.6%, the current dividend yield of 4% and the expected FFO/share growth rate of 13% to find the expected compound annual total return of 24.6%. That result is less than the DDM estimate, but with the relatively low risk, I’d be happy with either one. As a matter of fact, I’d be happy with a CAGR of half my calculated estimate.

The layering of future income that grows faster than the long-term rate of inflation is one of the primary areas of focus in my book, “Making Wall Street Irrelevant – Successful Investing Made Simple.”

Risks

Patent expirations are always a risk for any pharmaceutical company and Merck is no exception with its massive revenue generator, Keytruda, coming off patent in 2028 and its former leading vaccine, Gardasil, already losing market share. Fortunately, Merck has made great acquisitions and has a full pipeline of promising treatments to shore up future revenue generation for the future. The risk is that the promise of potential new drugs currently in trials do not live up to expectations.

Tariffs and government policies, domestic and abroad, have created uncertainties that, if I am wrong in my expectations, could create earnings headwinds for Merck and its competitors. I have been right so far and hope that the eventual outcomes are close to what I expect. If so, the impact of any changes in policy will be manageable by a behemoth like Merck.

Conclusion

My assessment of Merck appears to fall in line with all the ratings in the summary below.

In as far as the factor grades are concerned, I think everything provides and accurate portrayal of Merck at the moment. The stock is trading below fair value (though I’d probably rate this as an A rather than B+), the growth potential is an A, and profitability is A+. Momentum is lacking in the stock price and positive revisions outnumber negative ones, but just barely, so a C+ seems appropriate as well.

I’m going to go out on a limb just a little and rate Merck as a Strong Buy at its current price of $79.89. I think we can expect a total return CAGR in the vicinity of 25% through 2030 with very low risk relative to the overall market.

Full disclosure: I own Merck and am overweight in the healthcare sector because I expect above average appreciation over the next five years and strong dividend growth along with it. Much of the sector is undervalued relative to its historical mean and I expect that to swing in the other direction.

data.title: Alibaba: Surge In Stock Is Driven By Psychology, Not Fundamentals
data.description:
To describe the stock market in one sentence: the stock market is a discounting mechanism in which the market tries to estimate future cash flows and discounts them to the present using a proper risk-adjusted rate. These estimation games are the main culprit behind the significant volatility in company stock prices. Everyone who works for a corporation must agree on how hard it is to change a company’s strategic direction and how long it takes for those changes to affect the company’s bottom line. Looking at the recent surge in Alibaba stock (NYSE:BABA), what I see is a change in investor psychology based on the premise of future AI and cloud computing revenues, rather than on solid fundamentals. The first time I wrote an article about Alibaba was in August 2023, when I argued that the company was significantly undervalued on a sum-of-the-parts basis. This time, I revisited the investment case, but with a different conclusion.

In the past twelve months, Alibaba stock has advanced more than 96%, and including dividends, it has returned more than 100%. However, most of this growth was achieved since the announcement of the 1Q 2026 earnings report at the end of August. The key figure was a 26% year-over-year growth in Alibaba’s Cloud Intelligence Group revenues, which catapulted the stock after management’s remarks about focusing on cloud and AI.

Furthermore, during the earnings call, management announced strategic investments of RMB 380 billion (USD 54 billion) into its cloud intelligence and AI-related products, highlighting these as the company’s strategic pillars and its biggest long-term opportunities. The company’s issuance of USD 3.2 billion zero-coupon convertible debt for future investments in its cloud unit confirms the scale of investment required to build this nascent industry.

To supplement its cloud computing unit, Alibaba is continually improving its position in chip production. The company Semiconductor's design unit, T-Head, represents China’s response to achieving self-sufficiency in chip manufacturing. According to recent news, T- Head has developed a chip that is, in many respects, comparable to Nvidia’s H20. Alibaba’s ability to design more sophisticated chips demonstrates its strong position in the AI race between Western based companies and their Chinese counterparts. Additionally, the family of Large Language Models (LLM), Qwen, provides several open-sourced models that have seen widespread adoption both in China and overseas.

It is apparent that the market believes these massive investments in AI and cloud computing will turn Alibaba into a dominant player in this nascent industry. Furthermore, China’s regulatory ban on the use of Nvidia’s RTX Pro 6000D chips clears the way for homegrown alternatives, which only accentuates the bullish case for Alibaba.

However, looking at Alibaba’s investment case from a financial standpoint makes me question whether the market hasn’t gone too far with the AI narrative. Since there is so much focus and optimism around AI, there is a possibility that any disappointment in future developments could lead to a substantial pullback. Furthermore, if the significant investments in AI and cloud fail to translate into revenues and, subsequently, earnings, some investors might reconsider the current narrative. The main points why I think it is better to stay cautious are:

Cloud Intelligence Unit is still a small proportion of total Alibaba revenues

In 1Q 2026, Alibaba’s total revenues were RMB 247.6 billion, of which 57% came from the China E-commerce Group and only 13% from Cloud Intelligence. Even though the cloud segment grew 26% year-over-year compared to the 10% growth generated by China E-commerce, this additional growth is still too marginal to have a significant impact on total revenues. Additionally, the profitability of Cloud Intelligence, measured by EBITA, is still less than 8% of total EBITA generated. Put another way, EBITA generated by China E-commerce is 13 times larger than that of Cloud Intelligence. Lastly, Cloud Intelligence is a lower margin business compared to E-commerce. In 1Q 2026, it generated a 9% EBITA margin, compared to 27% for China E-commerce. While it is expected that the profitability of Cloud Intelligence will improve in the coming years, whether it can match the profitability of the E-commerce business remains an open question. All in all, higher growth in a lower margin business will put pressure on the total consolidated margin, which might negatively impact Alibaba’s valuation.

Strong competition in AI and cloud computing in China

Alibaba’s Qwen, Ten­cent’s Yuan­bao, Baidu’s Ernie X1.1 and DeepSeek are all Chinese-based AI models competing against each other as well as against their western counterparts. Product commoditization among Chinese LLM models is the main challenge to the future monetization of their AI solutions. Strong competition among Chinese peers is also apparent in their recently announced financing deals.

In September, Tencent announced plans to issue new bonds to fund its AI infrastructure. Tencent plans to issue the equivalent of USD 1.0 billion bond, split into three tranches with 5 year, 10 year and 30 year maturities. Baidu completed its bond offering of USD 2.0 billion in March of 2025 to supplement its AI CAPEX. What is typical for this group of Chinese hyperscalers - Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu, Huawai and Kingsoft Cloud - is the enormous capital intensity of their business models. That is a significant change in their business models, as these companies where initially asset-light businesses with little need for new capital.

It seems to me that the current AI investment frenzy is similar to major investment themes of the past, which were characterized by large capital inflows followed by price wars and, eventually, significant capital losses for the majority of investors. Recent history is full of big investment themes that turned sour. Consider industries such as EV production, solar panel manufacturing, battery manufacturing, or ride sharing. These were brilliant ideas with an amazing impact on real life, but they delivered poor returns for investors, as many of these investments did not turn into sustainable profits.

To support my arguments, let’s look at the developments in cloud infrastructure market shares in China between two periods: 1Q 2023 and 1Q 2025. While the ranking of top three players remains the same, during this period Alibaba lost 1% of market share, Huawei Cloud lost 2%, and Tencent lost 7%. On the other hand, the “Others” category – which includes Baidu - gained more than 10%. This category is mainly represented by China’s national telecom companies: China Unicom, China Mobile and China Telecom.

Even though it doesn’t seem probable that any of the national telcos could take substantial market share from Alibaba, it is possible that their market share will continue to grow at the expense of top three players in the future.

China Cloud Infrastructure market share 1Q 2025

China Cloud Infrastructure market share 1Q 2023

Valuation is not cheap

Alibaba trades at an 18.9 x price-to earnings (P/E) multiple compared to its five-year average of 13.1x. Similarly, the current Enterprise Value to EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) multiple is 14.3x, compared to its five-year average of 9.4x times. Even though multiples such as P/E or EV/EBITDA are not the most accurate metrics for estimating the value of a business, they are good measures of the sentiment incorporated into the stock price.

I believe that at the current price, Alibaba is neither undervalued nor cheap, and since its shares have advanced more than 92% year-to-date, I would prepare for a possible pullback rather than a continuation of the trend. However, it must be noted that expensive companies can become even more expensive, and Alibaba might not be an exception.

Summary

In recent months, Alibaba’s investment narrative has shifted from an e-commerce story to an AI and cloud intelligence story. Even though Alibaba’s business model hasn’t changed much in the past twelve months, investor psychology has changed significantly. However, this great optimism introduces room for error and disappointment. The Cloud Intelligence Unit is still a small portion of total revenues, and even strong demand for cloud services in the coming quarters or years may not be enough to move the needle. Additionally, as has been the case in other industries across China, strong capital inflows into AI and cloud services are creating a highly competitive environment, leading to price wars and subpar returns.

After Alibaba’s strong surge over the past twelve months, the stock is no longer undervalued, as it was for a long time, nor is it cheap. At its current valuation, Alibaba trades at a 44% premium to its five-year average P/E multiple. Even if multiples are not perfect metrics of value, they are solid indicators of sentiment - and that sentiment is undoubtedly optimistic. For me personally, Alibaba is a hold, but I am staying cautious. If exuberance in the shares continues, I will gradually reduce my position.

data.title: Delivery Hero: Operational Improvements But Still Many Uncertainties
data.description:
Introduction

My last article on Delivery Hero (OTCPK:DLVHF) dates back to February 2024, and I thought it was time to update the analysis given the various developments that have taken place. The previous article was part of a series of three in which I explored the food delivery sector in detail, so I will avoid repeating myself unnecessarily and invite you to read the previous articles if you want more in-depth information.

Since my last article, Delivery Hero's situation has improved, both in terms of capital and profitability. However, there are still some uncertainties regarding the business model of the sector in which it operates, which make Delivery Hero a risky company to invest in.

Business Model

In 2024, Delivery Hero successfully completed the transformation of its business into an integrated global platform. Using proprietary technology for logistics, payments, search, and customer care, integrated at a global level, it manages a group of platforms operating in over 70 countries for food delivery and quick commerce.

The group presents its results divided into five segments, four of which represent geographical areas: Asia, MENA, Europe, and the Americas; and the fifth is the segment dedicated to quick commerce: Integrated Verticals (Dmarts).

A significant change in the business model worth mentioning is the IPO of Talabat, a food delivery platform operating in the MENA region, which took place last year and raised €1.8 billion for Delivery Hero, which still retains an 80% stake. This was an excellent strategic choice, aimed at immediately enhancing the growth prospects and favorable business environment of Talabat, mainly due to weak labor laws and low-cost labor in the Middle East, which make the food delivery business much more profitable than in other geographical regions. The liquidity generated by the IPO was also necessary to stabilize the group's balance sheet.

In Spain, however, regulatory pressures have prompted the group to switch to a model of employed riders for Glovo, highlighting how regulatory pressure in Europe is a major weakness in the business model of these platforms.

Financial Performance

Between 2021 and 2024, Delivery Hero's revenue growth was excellent, although it slowed naturally after the COVID-related boom. The most positive aspect is the steady improvement in margins. Although positive EBIT levels have not yet been achieved, the operating loss has decreased significantly. The operating margin went from -28.3% in 2023 to -2.2% in 2024. There have also been improvements in solvency: at the end of 2023, the total debt/equity ratio was 342%, while at the end of the second half of the year it was 198%.

The first metrics that the company continues to present to shareholders are GMV, Total Segment Revenue, and Adjusted EBITDA; all metrics that exclude certain items from the calculation (such as share-based compensation). While the “adjusted” results are clearly highlighted on the second page of the report, to see the actual results achieved by the company, you have to go to page 7 of the report. For clarity, what you see indicated in the image as Total Segment Revenue is not the actual revenue generated by the company during the period, but rather the revenue before the reduction due to vouchers.

In the first half of 2025, Delivery Hero generated revenue of nearly €6.9 billion, up 19.2% year-on-year and exceeding GMV growth of only 3.9%. However, the costs incurred by the company to generate this revenue increased, and the gross profit margin was 25.2%, significantly lower than the 28.4% recorded in H1 2024. Nevertheless, a reduction in operating expenses, in particular a reduction of almost €300 million in general administrative expenses, led to a positive operating income of €4.7 million, a significant improvement on the previous year's €-390 million. However, the net result remains negative at €356 million (€-720 million in H1 2024). Free cash flow for the half-year was positive at €164.4 million, but net of extraordinary items, including Uber's breakup fee relating to the sale of Foodpanda Taiwan blocked by the authorities, this translates into -€7.7 million.

Over the last six months, the performance of the various geographical segments has shown very mixed trends, reflecting the specific operational and regulatory characteristics of each market.

MENA remains the strongest segment of the group, with a GMV of €7.2 billion and growth of 22.4%, revenues up 22.1% and positive EBITDA of approximately €256 million. In the Americas segment, however, we saw a significant reversal of the trend. GMV grew by 20.9% to €2 billion, with revenues up 18.3%. EBITDA returned to positive territory at €46.2 million, thanks mainly to the boost from subscriptions and the development of quick commerce.

The geographical areas with the worst performance were Asia and Europe. In Asia, GMV fell to €10.6 billion (-10.4% yoy). However, revenues grew by 10.5%, mainly thanks to the increase in the penetration of the proprietary delivery model, which rose from 52% to 69% in one year, and the offer of additional services. EBITDA improved to €176.3 million. The regional strengthening strategy was further consolidated with the acquisition of Deliveroo's assets in Hong Kong.

Europe remains the most complex geographical area. Although GMV increased by 11.6% to €4.8 billion and revenue rose by +29.7%, the segment recorded a negative EBITDA of -€50.8 million, a deterioration compared to 2024. The main critical issue is attributable to the increase in costs related to the transition to the model of employed riders in Spain, which weighed on margins. Finally, the Integrated Verticals segment continued its expansion, with GMV growing by 24.5% to €1.7 billion and EBITDA losses reduced to €17.3 million, compared to €73.8 million in 2024.

As of June 30, 2025, the group's liquidity stood at €2.8 billion, down from €3.8 billion in the previous year due to the repurchase of convertible bonds worth approximately €896 million. As a result, non-current debt decreased by 17% and shareholders' equity amounted to €2.3 billion (€2.7 billion in 2024).

Valuation

14 analysts expect Delivery Hero to reach €17.2 billion in revenue in 2027, while only three have also provided estimates for EBIT (€1.5 billion in 2027). The image also shows analysts' estimates for 2028, but these were provided by only two analysts and should therefore be treated with caution. Revenue growth is expected to continue, albeit at a moderate pace and at a slower rate than in the past, with a significant improvement in margins.

I tried to evaluate Delivery Hero using three scenarios, with very different assumptions given the high uncertainty and difficulty in predicting the company's future margins.

I adopted an equity side approach and, to determine the cost of equity, I started with a risk-free rate of 2.75% (10-year BUND), an equity risk premium of 4.33% (source: Damodaran), and a beta of 1.36. Based on these figures, I estimated a cost of equity of 8.64%. However, in my valuation, I will use a discount rate of 10%, as this represents the minimum return I want to obtain from an investment in a single stock. Finally, since I do not believe that Delivery Hero will distribute dividends in the coming years, the present value is influenced solely by the terminal value. I have therefore limited myself to estimating revenue, net income, and a possible P/E ratio for 2031. These are my assumptions for the different scenarios:

  • BASE CASE: Revenue of €20.3 billion in 2031; 6% net margin; P/E 18; Intrinsic value of approximately €10 billion.
  • BEST CASE: Revenue of €22.5 billion in 2031; 7,5% net margin; P/E 22; Intrinsic value of approximately €17 billion.
  • WORST CASE: Revenue of €17.3 billion in 2031; 5% net margin; P/E 15; Intrinsic value of approximately €6 billion.

Conclusion

Delivery Hero appears to be a company in transition, with encouraging signs in terms of growth and improved margins; however, the sector in which it operates is extremely competitive and I do not believe it is particularly attractive for investment. The reduction in leverage, more efficient cash management, and partial recovery of operating losses are evidence of good corporate governance. There are still some areas of weakness, primarily the heterogeneity of performance and the Asian segment, which is particularly weak compared to the past and has a significant impact on the group's results.

I find it extremely difficult to estimate what this company's future margins might be. The estimates I have used to evaluate the company are based on analysts' estimates; however, we would need to see a few fiscal semesters or years with a positive net margin to get a clearer idea of the company's potential. At the time of writing, the market capitalization is approximately €7.7 billion. In my worst-case scenario, the company is worth €6 billion, so it could seem like an attractive investment at these prices. In conclusion, I believe Delivery Hero is correctly valued, although I think there are currently better investment opportunities in companies that have a profitable track record and operate in more mature sectors with less regulatory pressure.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: A Turnaround Story Priced To Perfection
data.description:
The discussion about whether Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is overvalued or undervalued is highly heated. On the one hand, the EV business is under pressure, with stagnating sales in 2024 and even double-digit declines in Q2 2025. Moreover, regulatory credit revenue, which accounts for around 40% of net income, is at risk of disappearing entirely by 2027. On the other hand, some investors argue that Tesla is not merely an EV company but will play a leading role in the robotics and AI age.

In my view, the stock has become completely detached from reality. It is the only turnaround play I know that is priced to perfection. For me, Tesla is a Sell.

Musk's vision

The discussion around Tesla is heated. Elon Musk has created the narrative that Tesla is much more than just a car manufacturer - it is a tech company and will lead in FSD and robotaxis, as well as in robotics. The bears point to Musk's tendency to overpromise and underdeliver. For example, in 2019 he promised one million robotaxis by the end of 2020.

I want to understand the narrative, as it seems to drive Tesla's valuation.

First, full self-driving (FSD) and robotaxis.I have often read about the narrative of Tesla capturing a big chunk of the gigantic robotaxi market. When I dug deeper into it, I discovered the actual expected size of this particular new market. The U.S. robotaxi market is projected to grow to $43.8 billion by 2030. Even if Tesla captures 30% of this market and we assume a profit margin of 15%, this would result in only $2 billion of additional net income by 2030. Compared with the $1.3 trillion market cap, this doesn't excite me at all.

I see another issue with the technology Tesla uses. Elon Musk decided that Tesla would focus on a vision-only system for FSD. His reasoning was that humans also drive with their two eyes on the road. This comes at a cost advantage. Using vision only requires cameras, not expensive sensors, radars, and other technology. Cheaper cars make it easier for Tesla to scale. The Cybercab is estimated to cost between $20,000 and $25,000. Waymo, on the other hand, could have a final cost of $75,000. Now, a fully equipped vehicle costs around $150,000.

However, do customers really want to ride in a robotaxi that may be cheap but unsafe? The technology such as LiDAR, which Waymo - probably Tesla's biggest competitor in this field - is using, is active rather than passive. Therefore, it is able to see through certain things that cameras simply can't. For example, Tesla's FSD has difficulties distinguishing between a billboard and the sky or between crossing trucks.

Moreover, Tesla's FSD is still at level two, which means it requires a human supervisor. Another indicator that Tesla still has a long way to go is the accident rate, which is the highest of any auto brand.

The second and even bigger narrative is robotics. Tesla's humanoid robot Optimus is estimated to cost around $25,000, which would also make it affordable for many middle-class households. Nevertheless, the recently released new pay package for Musk aims for 100 million Optimus robots to be sold annually. I wondered if there is even enough demand for so many robots. If every American had one of these robots, which is unlikely, as not everyone can afford $25,000, it would take just 3.5 years until the market in the U.S. is saturated.

Musk stated that Optimus could generate 80% of Tesla's revenue in the future. In my view, the path for Tesla to reach 100 million units is still not clear enough. If Tesla somehow manages to achieve this target, it would completely transform the company. However, it sounds like science fiction and is very reminiscent of Tesla's ambitious goal, which was dropped last year, of reaching 20 million vehicles sold annually, which is more than ten times the current figure.

I believe that it is still too early to say whether Tesla can truly be successful in both areas. For FSD and robotaxis, it seems to me that Tesla has only the price advantage compared to competitors such as Waymo, while the technology lags behind. Still, I'm cautious to call Waymo the winner of the race, as technology can advance faster than expected. If Tesla somehow manages to improve its FSD while maintaining these costs per car, it could emerge as a leader in FSD.

Moreover, I don't see Tesla achieving the 100 million per year target for Optimus. I do believe, however, that Tesla could advance faster than expected and actually sell some of these robots - but certainly not 100 million, in my view.

Honestly, I don't think it is possible to reliably estimate the revenues and cash flows of a business or industry that does not yet really exist. Any attempt would be little more than a wild guess regarding margins, market size, or adoption. The way the market prices these innovations as if they will happen with certainty is what makes me bearish.

From Vision to Reality

The gap between Tesla's visionary narrative and its current business reality is widening. While Musk promises breakthroughs in autonomy and robotics, the automotive segment is under pressure, and fading regulatory credits pose a significant threat to profitability.

The automobile business

Negative comments often neglect the role Tesla played in transforming the automobile industry. Two decades ago, electric vehicles were not associated with being cool, fast, or stylish. Musk and his team changed this perception with the release of the Roadster. This put pressure on legacy automakers such as Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz, and others. Entering a high-CapEx business and scaling is extremely challenging. However, past results do not guarantee future returns, as the current situation clearly demonstrates.

In 2024, Tesla's automobile sales declined by 6%, from $82.42 billion to $77.07 billion. Looking at Q2 2025 alone, the decline is even more severe, at 16%. This is particularly concerning for Tesla, as automotive revenues still accounted for around 73% of total sales in the first half of the year.

I don't know a single automobile company that does not have an EV range or is not currently developing one. In 2015, Tesla was in second place worldwide by the number of cars sold. In 2017, Tesla finally became number one, selling more than 100,000 EVs. The company produced 410,000 cars in Q2 2025 alone. However, if you look at deliveries, you can see a 13% decline in the second quarter.

Tesla has been the EV market leader for years. However, BYD surpassed Tesla for the first time in 2024. BYD's sales jumped 60% in the first three months of 2025, while Tesla saw a decline. To illustrate rising competition, Tesla's market share dropped below its 2017 level of 40 percent, when the company ramped up Model 3 production. The once 80 percent market share has now fallen to 38 percent in the U.S. as of August. It is not entirely fair to compare the current market share with the 80 percent peak. However, the steep drop from over 48 percent in June to 38 percent in August is alarming. It gives the impression that Tesla has lost control and that competitors are eating its lunch. Additionally, Tesla's growth slowed to 3.1 percent while the broader market grew by 14 percent in August. It is clear that Tesla's main issue is not macroeconomic conditions but rising competition.

First, Tesla has not launched a new model since the Cybertruck in 2023. The other four models (3, Y, S, X) were released years ago. I believe that other vehicles from European EV legacy companies, as well as Asian challengers such as BYD, offer comparable quality. According to the annual influential study conducted by J.D. Power, Tesla has lost its lead over legacy automakers in the quality of its new all-electric vehicles. The number of problems reported per 100 newly sold or leased vehicles was 266, the same as its competitors. Moreover, Tesla currently does not offer a cheaper version of the Model 3 and is losing market share to more affordable EVs. What I find impressive is that Tesla can produce electric vehicles at a lower cost than almost any competitor. The above-average margins are certainly why Tesla has been able to engage in price competition. However, the gross profit margin on automotive revenues has contracted from over 25 percent in 2022 to 16.7 percent in the first half of 2025.Nevertheless, the price cuts in the U.S., China, and Germany indicate that competitors can still offer lower prices. For example, BYD's entry-level model sells in China for just under $10,000. Tesla's Model 3 is its cheapest vehicle, but costs roughly three times more.

Regulatory credit could reduce profits significantly

Each company has an annual quota of credits it needs to meet. However, not every automaker sells enough EVs to reach this quota and must purchase credits from companies that have generated a surplus. Companies like Tesla and Rivian, which produce only EVs, typically have a surplus they can sell.

Regulatory credit revenue is particularly attractive because it is essentially 100% margin, directly enhancing profit. In 2024, roughly 40% of Tesla's net income came from these credits, and in the first half of 2025, this share was even 65%. Historically, when Tesla's core business was not yet profitable, these regulatory credits helped the company achieve profitability. They helped offset some margin pressure from price cuts.

Tesla generated over $2.7 billion in regulatory credit revenue in 2024 alone. However, in Q2 2025, investors saw a drop of more than 50%, down to $438 million, and for the first half of the year, a decline of around 23% to just over $1 billion. Keep in mind that revenue from this segment can fluctuate a lot from quarter to quarter. Tesla usually earns more credit revenue in the second half of the year.

What I personally dislike about this segment is that it boosts Tesla's operating profit without reflecting production or innovation by the company itself. In my view, it is closer to financial income. Regulatory credit revenue is not sustainable and will likely decline significantly in the coming years.

First, more automakers will meet their credit quotas. This will reduce the demand for credits and put pressure on Tesla's margins.

Second, the Trump administration takes a different direction than the Biden administration.Trump has pushed to revoke a federal waiver that allows California to set its own vehicle emission standards. Moreover, recent legislation could eliminate fines for automakers that fail to meet environmental targets.Both factors would negatively affect the demand for regulatory credits.

If Trump had not been president, Tesla could have expected regulatory credit sales to remain strong, similar to 2024, as stricter quotas would have increased both demand and prices for these credits. Wall Street currently expects this year's regulatory credit revenue to decline by 21% to $2.17 billion, with further decreases in the coming years. The most pessimistic forecast comes from William Blair, which projects $1.5 billion in 2025, $595 million in 2026, and zero in 2027.

I agree with William Blair's view. I do not believe the Trump administration is likely to continue supporting regulatory credits. Of course, Tesla still earns some credits in Europe and China; however, legacy EV automakers are accelerating their transition to electric vehicles, which will further reduce Tesla's relative advantage in this area.

Tesla reported a net income of $7.13 billion in 2024. I assume the declining automobile business and all other segments to be constant. Net income would be around $5.93 billion in 2025, approximately $5 billion in 2026, and $4.43 billion in 2027.

I believe this illustrates that Tesla is currently profitable as it stands. However, profits could decline by nearly 40% over the next 2.5 years if revenues and margins in the other segments remain constant.

While there are potential additional revenue streams, I believe it will take time for them to generate sufficient profits to offset the short-term impact of the sharply declining regulatory credit revenue. As discussed earlier, the automobile segment alone will likely not be enough to sustain growth in the near future.

Valuation

Sum of parts

The perhaps most challenging part of valuing Tesla is the combination of a declining business, a growing business, and a currently non-existent but potentially promising business.

It is not easy to put a number on Tesla's automotive business. If we compare Tesla with BYD, we can see that the gross margins of both companies are very similar. I apply a slightly higher rate for Tesla to reflect the missing China risk premium. BYD's forward Price/Sales is 1.01 and I will apply 1.3 for Tesla.

In 2024, total automotive revenues were $77.07 billion. As the tax credit of $7,500 expires on September 30, 2025, we could see stronger sales in Q3.

I assume a revenue drop of more than 10% for the full year, as sales have already declined by 20% in Q1 2025 and by 16% in Q2 2025. I expect Tesla's sales to be better in Q3, as customers may want to buy EVs before the $7,500 tax credit expires.

Applying this number and a Price/Sales ratio of 1.3 gives a value of the automotive business of $90.17 billion. Note that I use less conservative assumptions here, considering the 16% sales drop in Q2 and relatively stabilized margins.

The automobile business is therefore approximately worth $90.17 billion. Adding the expected $1.5 billion from regulatory credits, according to William Blair, and $1.8 billion from automotive leasing results (which I assume will stay constant for simplicity) brings the total to $93.47 billion. Keep in mind, however, that regulatory credits are likely to decline to zero by 2027.

As a note: estimating growth in the EV business is challenging, so I use this shortcut based on this year's expected revenue. While not entirely accurate, it provides a reasonable approximation.

The market capitalization is still over 13 times larger than my calculated value of the automotive business.

Tesla Energy is a rapidly growing business.

The energy business has shown impressive growth over the past three years, along with margin improvement. Revenue increased from $3.9 billion in 2022, with a gross margin of around 7%, to over $10 billion in 2024, with a gross margin of 26%.

If we take a closer look, the 10-K shows that the segment benefited from manufacturing credits of $756 million. This partially offset a 52% increase in the cost of revenue for generation and storage. This is reminiscent of the regulatory credits discussed earlier. It would be incorrect to assume these credits will continue indefinitely, especially since the Trump administration plans to reduce tax relief for renewable energy under its proposed "Big Beautiful Bill."

If we add the $756 million back to the gross profit, we get a revised gross margin of just under 20%. However, I believe that with increasing scale, margin expansion to 25% is possible in the long term.

In my mid-case scenario, I assume a gross margin of 22% and an average revenue growth of 40% over the next five years. My gross margin assumption accounts for a loss in manufacturing credits, and my revenue growth rate is, for simplicity, slightly above the 37% three-year CAGR. This results in a gross profit of $11.9 billion and revenue of $54.24 billion by 2029. The business would be worth $88 billion today.

If we add the value of the automotive business of $93.47 billion (including regulatory credits) to the energy business at $88 billion, we arrive at a total of $181.47 billion.

The third segment, Services, generated $10.53 billion in revenue in 2024. The gross margin was only 6%, compared to over 17% for the EV business and 25% for the Energy segment. However, this segment has been growing rapidly, from $6.091 billion in 2022 to $10.53 billion in 2024. In Q2 2025, the Services segment was the only one showing growth, at 17%.

I expect an average annual growth rate of 13% for this segment, to be more conservative compared to the 17% growth in Q2, while maintaining the same 6% gross margin. I apply a Price/Sales ratio of 0.85, which reflects the lower margin compared to the automobile business, yet remains above the industry average revenue multiple of 0.64 for the auto service and repair industry. I value the Services business at approximately $10.72 billion.

Adding this to the previously valued segments, Tesla's existing business is worth roughly $192 billion. I have used conservative assumptions. If the automotive business continues to decline and the Energy business grows more slowly than expected, the total value could be below my $192 billion estimate. Conversely, if the automotive business stabilizes or recovers and the Energy business grows faster than projected, my estimate could be too low. Therefore, I believe the overall business could reasonably be valued between $160 billion and $220 billion. This wide range reflects the inherent challenge of valuing Tesla, as its future direction is uncertain.

Nevertheless, the current market capitalization is more than six times larger than the estimated value of Tesla's existing business.

DCF

I prefer to take a conservative approach, avoiding overly optimistic assumptions that could make the business appear undervalued when it actually isn't. To project future cash flows, I use the FCF margin, multiplied by projected (growing) revenue. This year, the FCF margin is expected to be 6%, which I also use as my base-case assumption. The six-year average was 6.67%, and the median 6.65%.

My discount rate of 9% reflects my opportunity cost. I account for risk not by adjusting the discount rate but by applying a margin of safety. The more uncertain I consider a business, the higher the margin of safety I require. For Tesla, I would prefer a margin of safety of at least 35%, given the cyclicality of the automobile industry, the risk of losing market share to competitors, brand damage, and the fading regulatory credit revenue. These are the primary sources of uncertainty. Nevertheless, Tesla has a healthy balance sheet and has repeatedly demonstrated its ability to turn the business around and withstand hardship.

To estimate the terminal value, I use the long-term GDP growth rate plus inflation, which I assume to be 2.5%.

Just to be clear, I am being generous in this DCF model. I do not assume further deterioration of Tesla's EV business in the mid-case scenario, and I still assume 40% annual revenue growth for the energy segment. On average, I assume overall revenue growth of 15%.

In my bad-case scenario, the fair value is $15.36 per share, in my mid-case scenario $30.73, and in my best-case scenario $35.85. The current market price of $420 per share is nowhere near these levels. The DCF is less generous than the sales multiple approach, but free cash flow matters more for investors than revenue growth.

The interesting part is to determine the growth rate necessary to justify the current valuation. If revenue grows on average by 50% after a flat year in 2026, the fair value in my bad-case and mid-case scenarios rises to $40.03 and $80.07, respectively. Even then, the current stock price of $420 is far above the fair value.

To justify the current market cap, Tesla's revenues would need to grow at an average annual rate of 50%, and the FCF margin would have to be five times higher than it currently is. And all of that just to justify the current stock price.

Risks

My thesis that Tesla is a very expensive turnaround play, and that revenues and profits from robotics, FSD, and robotaxis are highly uncertain, could be wrong for the following reasons.

First, the automobile business could recover significantly. Tesla released an updated version of the Model Y, called Juniper. The Model Y was Tesla's best-selling car in 2023 and a huge success. The updated interior, performance, and design make the new version of the Model Y appealing and could attract more customers. Moreover, there are rumors about a second-generation Tesla Roadster in 2026. Honestly, I believe the car looks pretty cool; however, Elon Musk has often overpromised in the past. I believe the Roadster will arrive in maybe two to three years, but I have my doubts about the ambitious target to ramp up production next year. Another rumor concerns the $25,000 Model 2, which would be competitive with BYD's EVs. If Tesla manages to actually produce the vehicle, they could regain market share. However, they have already promised the Model 2 since 2020.

Secondly, Tesla could become the leader in robotics and full self-driving. If this happens, and the overall TAM increases, as well as if there are more use cases for robots, this could be a significant win for Tesla shareholders who bought the stock in April or May. However, at current prices, the narrative is, in my view, already fully reflected in the share price.

Summary

Tesla has already transformed one industry, but it would now need to essentially create two completely new ones: humanoid robotics and robotaxis. I assume Tesla shareholders place a great deal of faith in Mr. Musk's ability to innovate and his brilliant mind. However, this is the same mind that has recently put Tesla in serious trouble.

I don't believe anyone can reliably value technological development at such an early stage. I certainly cannot. Who knows how large the robotaxi or humanoid robotics markets will become? Simply assuming that Tesla will dominate all these fields because of one man who splits his attention across multiple ventures and is far from infallible is risky. Tesla has a history of overpromising and underdelivering.

Tesla is not an investment; it is speculation. I wouldn't be surprised if the stock doubles from here, as it is completely detached from reality. Profiting from Tesla is largely a matter of luck. There are far more attractive investments and more exciting speculative opportunities elsewhere in the market.

It's a Sell for me.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Applied Digital: Gigawatts War Could Get Messy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ:APLD) is a digital infrastructure stock that caters specifically to the HPC and AI sectors in North America, meaning it has positioned itself to benefit from the massive build-out in AI infrastructure underway. Similar to IREN (IREN), the company transitioned from a blockchain-focused data center to an AI infrastructure play, and the market loves it.

The stock is up +225% since the start of the year, recovering from April lows of $3.44 to $25.40 per share at market open on Tuesday and closing at $23.45. Valuation also reflects the stock’s new AI status with EV/Sales of 44x versus a sector average of 4x. The most recent leg in Applied Digital’s winning streak is on the back of an expanded data center lease agreement with CoreWeave (CRWV) for a new 150MW data center in the Polaris Forge 1 Campus, which brings Applied Digital’s total contracted capacity up to 400MW and an estimated $11B in lease revenue down the line for the three agreements in place. The first agreement, from May, was for a 100MW data center, while the second was 150MW. The timeline is that the 100MW data center should be ready by 4Q25, while the second 150MW will be operational in mid-2026.

It’s clear to those watching Applied Digital that the stock moves in reaction to data center agreements; the stock tends to shoot up on news of expanded build-out before letting off steam (which we saw on Wednesday with the stock trading down around 5% plus another 2% down in pre-market trading Thursday morning). The stock is benefiting from growing momentum around the AI infrastructure build-out this quarter, particularly with the expanded CoreWeave deal and news of Microsoft (MSFT) and Nebius (NBIS) making a deal that runs through 2031, where Nebius provides Microsoft with dedicated GPU capacity. More and more money is being poured into AI infrastructure all around: OpenAI signing a $300B deal with Oracle, Nvidia investing $100B in the OpenAI data center buildout, and Nvidia also buying extra capacity from CoreWeave.

It feels unlikely that we’ll keep this spending up, considering that at the end of the day, there’s only so many cloud dollars you can spend on compute power before needing to justify the spend in material returns. As of now, the only players seeing such returns are Big Tech, i.e., Meta Platforms and Google, among others. It's crucial for investors to differentiate between companies that benefit from AI for their internal business to boost top-line growth and those that benefit from the former group's spending on compute. The former group can’t outspend their returns for long, and the latter is vulnerable to any moderation in spending on compute.

So, if we assume that CapEx for compute power doesn’t grow on trees, then it’s a matter of how much more capacity Applied Digital has to lease out and market expectations as the year progresses.

1. Capacity available to lease out:

As of now, Applied Digital has the Polaris Forge in North Dakota with four campuses that are in different phases of development. The two that investors should care about now are the Polaris Forge 1 or Ellendale campus, which is already active and being used for CoreWeave's lease of 400MW, and the Polaris Forge 2, which has 280MW of initial planned capacity and is expected to be operational in 2026 and at full capacity in 2027. The other two campuses are in the planning stage. This brings the total for Applied Digital to around 480MW constructed data center capacity, 400MW that’s being built out right now and is not yet online, and a 1.4GW pipeline, which is capacity that is being planned but not yet under construction. This means with the CoreWeave deal, Applied Digital could still be able to lease out another 400MW as a best-case scenario in 2026.

In our view, Applied Digital stock will move up on to another deal, and the odds of this happening could be high. Management noted that it's in discussions with a U.S.-based hyperscaler. We still don’t know who the mystery hyperscaler could be, but we do think it’s likely that they’ll be locking in capacity that isn’t yet online. We believe that the lease will be from one of the following companies: Meta Platforms, Microsoft, OpenAI, Amazon, or Anthropic. It’s unlikely to see players outside this list sign a multi-billion dollar contract on compute. We think it's likely to see another deal come through, but that alone doesn't justify adding the stock at current levels considering that the market has priced in a lot of the positives already.

2. Market expectations

The danger about companies like Applied Digital is the fact that the market is leaving them with little to no margin for error or moderation in spending. The stock bounced on the company’s Q4 earnings results, reported in late July, topping Street estimates with revenue of $38M, up 41% Y/Y and outpacing estimates for $37.1M and adjusted loss per share of $0.3, better than the expected loss of $15. The market doesn’t seem to mind that Applied Digital is still pre-profitable with high CapEx to fuel its large-scale build-out. This setup means there is a high risk of execution error, which the market isn’t pricing in. It seems that markets are expecting increased AI infrastructure efforts to keep trickling and benefit guys like Applied Digital. Aside from the potential moderation of spend, the other problem we see is that the market is acting like Applied Digital is the only game in town, but in reality, it’s one of many players trying to get in on the AI hype. The hard pill to swallow is the fact that not all these players can be winners.

The three-month chart above shows a couple of the main players: Equinix, CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, and, of course, Applied Digital. The stock is second only to Equinix, up 127% over the past three months and 47% over the past month. All these players (in addition to others) are building out capacity for increasing compute needs: CoreWeave has about 470MW in active power as of 2Q25 with contracted capacity of 2,200MW and new plans for a Pennsylvania build with potential capacity of 300MW; Equinix is targeting north of 725MW with its xScale hyperscale ventures and adding more retail capacity; Nebius has 300MW in New Jersey, an expanding Finland facility to 25MW to 75MW; IREN has 2,910MW in capacity. So, the competition is tough, and there is a cap on how much capacity we need in the near to mid-term.

Bottom Line:

Applied Digital RSI has been comfortably within overbought territory since the 12th of September, and is currently at 75.87. The stock continues to be volatile in spite of the overall upward trajectory, with 96 moves of over 5% over the last 12 months. The stock is entering a higher risk profile, in our opinion, after the run-up, and the question is whether this is a longer-term winner or an overcrowded AI play.

Applied Digital could be on the verge of signing a huge deal with a hyperscaler to lease out data center capacity that’s not yet online. There is little doubt that this would push the stock past its current all-time high of $25.61 per share. Having said that, we do think this is speculative at best and too risky to add at current levels. Hence, we see an opportunity for investors to trim their holdings heavily at current levels.

data.title: Paypal Is Pulling An Apple, Making It A Buy
data.description:
PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) has not lived up to the expectations of investors over the past five years. After losing ~60% of its market value since September 2020, the company is among the very few profitable tech giants to have lost ground in the post-pandemic era. Revenue growth has fallen off a cliff (from 20.7% in 2020 to just 6.8% in 2024), the loss of eBay's (EBAY) business since mid-2020 has continued to put pressure on growth, and the competition in the payment processing industry has intensified notably amid changing spending patterns of global consumers. Mr. Market has noticed all these challenges. What has gone unnoticed, in my opinion, is how PayPal is transitioning from a high-growth business to a more mature, cash-rich, slow-growth business. I believe the lackluster performance of PYPL stock in the past five years has come on the back of PayPal failing to live up to its standards as a high-growth business. Now that the economic reality facing PayPal has changed, I believe it's time to appropriately value PayPal as the cash-rich, mature business it is. For the first time in many years, I am turning bullish on PayPal based on the findings of this analysis.

PayPal Is Taking A Leaf Out Of Apple's Playbook

I believe PayPal is well-positioned to follow the footsteps of Apple, Inc. (AAPL). Not by venturing into the smartphone industry, of course, but by focusing on distributing wealth to shareholders while boosting EPS in the process. As illustrated below, Apple has retired approximately two billion shares in the past five years alone.

Exhibit 1: Apple shares outstanding

Between 2018 and 2020, Apple's EPS growth was primarily driven by a reduction in the share count, not meaningful growth in net income. In fact, between Fiscal 2018 and 2020, net income declined from $59.5 billion to $57.41 billion while diluted EPS increased from $2.98 to $3.28. This playbook enabled Apple to enjoy share price appreciation even during a time when earnings were flat.

Today, PayPal has found itself in a somewhat similar situation. The company, after years of stellar double-digit growth through 2021, is now experiencing a notable deceleration in revenue growth. At this transitory business phase, PayPal seems to be focused on boosting shareholder returns through buybacks. PayPal's board of directors authorized a massive $15 billion share repurchase program last February, and at the end of Q2, ~$2 billion in repurchase authorization was still available from the previous program as well. This brings the current total repurchase authorization to ~17 billion. As illustrated below, PayPal's outstanding share count has gradually declined in the past few years, which suggests the company is thoughtfully executing these buybacks as a long-term strategy to boost EPS while distributing excess cash to shareholders.

Exhibit 2: PayPal outstanding shares

In order to make sure that PayPal's management is completely on board with this strategy, I had a look at the management commentary in the past few Annual Reports. What I found was clear evidence of this strategy. In the 2024 Annual Report, the company provided color on its value creation goals, and commenting on capital allocation, wrote:

Distribute up to 70-80% of FCF annually, with near-term focus on stock buybacks.

Exhibit 3: PayPal's value creation goals

This was confirmed by CFO Jamie Miller on the Q2 earnings call as well. What is encouraging to see is that PayPal is focused on funding these buybacks from cash generated from its business operations. Over a long enough time period, this strategy should materially de-risk investing in PayPal.

The next important question is, can PayPal continue with this massive distribution of wealth to shareholders for a long period of time? As I discuss in the next segment, I believe PayPal has what it takes to do this.

PayPal's FCF Outlook Remains Bright

From a cash flow generation perspective, a few things are working well for PayPal. Probably the most important factor is PayPal's asset-light business model. PayPal, unlike manufacturing businesses, does not hold any inventory. The company, through its digital payment platforms, charges a fee for enabling seamless B2C and peer-to-peer transactions, which is a high-margin business. The capital light business model makes it even better. PayPal does not incur significant investment outlays regularly, helping the company convert a high percentage of its operating cash flows into FCF.

Year Net cash from operations CapEx FCF conversion rate
2024 $7.14 billion $0.8 billion 89%
2023 $4.8 billion $1 billion 79%
2022 $5.8 billion $0.9 billion 84%

Source: Company filings and author's calculations

In addition to the prevalence of high FCF conversion rates, I am encouraged by the new strategic direction under CEO Alex Chriss where the company is trying to focus on cash flow sustainability and earnings growth, not growth at any cost. Historically, PayPal investors and analysts have focused heavily on TPVs reported by the company. Under the new CEO, PayPal has pivoted to focus more on transaction margin dollars which is calculated by subtracting transaction-related expenses from revenue. This new strategic direction forces the company to focus more on high-margin business opportunities rather than trying to grow the total value of transactions. In the long run, this strategy is likely to help PayPal focus on high-quality, durable business opportunities that boost the overall profitability of the business. PayPal is also focused on cost reduction to boost profit margins.

The SA analyst community seems to be bullish on PayPal so a lot has already been written on PayPal's cheap valuation and prospects so I am not going to repeat them here to keep the length of this analysis in check. For ease of reference, I will highlight the reasons why I believe PayPal has positioned itself to see stable revenue growth in the long run.

  1. PayPal's recent investments in growing the adoption of its branded checkout button with a focus on competing with the likes of Apple Pay.
  2. Localized checkout experience through PayPal World.
  3. Focus on transaction margin dollars.
  4. The ongoing Venmo monetization.
  5. Focus on SMBs with tools such as PayPal Zettle.
  6. Recent investments in AI to roll out AI features including agentic commerce.
  7. The strategic decision to embrace the growing adoption of digital currencies with the launch of PYUSD.

In summary, I am confident that PayPal's cash-generation machine will continue to operate smoothly in the foreseeable future, giving the company leeway to aggressively execute its buyback strategy, eventually boosting EPS.

Risks

My thesis for PayPal hinges on the success of its buyback program. Because of this, I am keeping a close eye on operating profit margins. I believe investors should monitor how PayPal's strategic investments in AI, the core business, and new initiatives such as stablecoins impact operating margins both in the short and long term. Any permanent deterioration in margins will force me to reconsider my bullish thesis. I am also monitoring the global digital payments landscape actively as rapid technological advancements have paved the way for disruptive technologies to displace traditional modes of payment.

Takeaway

PayPal seems to have taken a page out of Apple's capital allocation playbook to drive EPS higher through aggressive buybacks. I am on board with this strategy as I believe it is an efficient use of capital given PayPal's scale and the maturing core market opportunity. Encouragingly, I have gathered enough evidence to support my belief that PayPal is not sitting idle either, by actively pursuing new opportunities to grow while focusing on returning capital to shareholders. The new PayPal business model deserves a valuation re-rating in the market, in my opinion.

data.title: Keros Therapeutics: A Potential Dart Throw
data.description:
Shares of biopharmaceutical concern Keros Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:KROS) are trading at a small discount to cash after officially abandoning its cibotercept program, leaving it one wholly owned asset. With a 2025 that has so far featured a restructuring, activist investor intrigue, and a strategic review, Keros is forging ahead with its KER-065 program that should enter Phase 2 study in Q1 '26. With $1.1 billion of potential milestones from a licensing deal with Takeda (TAK) and a promised return of $375 million to shareholders, this small cap merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Keros Therapeutics, Inc. is a Lexington, Massachusetts-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of therapies that target disorders linked to dysfunctional signaling of transforming growth factor-betas (TGF-ß). The company is advancing one wholly owned early-stage clinical program (KER-065) against Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) and has out-licensed its late-stage cytopenia candidate (elritercept) to Takeda. Keros was formed in 2015 and went public in 2020, raising net proceeds of $100.1 million at $16 per share. The stock currently trades around $15.75 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $640 million.

TGF-ß

TGF-ß are a family of proteins that function as master regulators of growth, repair, and maintenance in several tissues, including blood, bone, heart, skeletal muscle, and adipose. The company has leveraged its TGF-ß expertise into two active clinical programs.

Pipeline

KER-065. The company’s new raison d’etre and only wholly owned asset is KER-065, a modified ligand trap that binds to TGF-ß superfamily receptors known as activin receptor type IIA and type IIB (ActRIIA and ActRIIB). They inhibit TGF-ß ligands myostatin (GDF8) and activin A, which are negative regulators of mass and strength in muscles and bones. As such, KER-065 is thought to have potential to not only improve musculoskeletal outcomes, but also increase energy expenditure, reduce body fat, lower insulin resistance, and improve cardiac function, rendering it a potential complement to the white-hot glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist obesity therapies. It is also designed to reduce binding to bone morphogenic proteins to avoid vascular and blood loss side effects.

In a Phase 1 study involving 38 healthy male volunteers, KER-065 was generally well-tolerated with no bleeding or a specific blood vessel dilation disorder (telangiectasias) observed. Furthermore, the ligand trap increased whole body bone mineral density, increased muscle mass, and decreased visceral fat mass at day 85. Based on those encouraging results that could positively impact a broad range of neuromuscular diseases, Keros elected DMD as its first indication to target in a Phase 2 trial. The company will huddle with regulators in 3Q25 with the hopes of initiating the study in 1Q26. KER-065 received Orphan Disease designation from the FDA for DMD on August 20, 2025.

Elritercept (KER-050). Until December 2024, Keros’ lead product candidate was elritercept, an engineered ligand trap that binds to ActRIIA, with an endgame of promoting the production of red blood cells and platelets. As such, it is being advanced for the treatment of low blood cell counts (cytopenias) due to ineffective hematopoiesis (blood cell formation), including anemia and thrombocytopenia in patients with myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) or rare bone cancer myelofibrosis (MF).

Those clinical studies are now being conducted by Takeda, which entered into a global licensing deal (ex-Mainland China, Hong Kong, and Macau) with Keros for elritercept in December 2024. Under the terms of the agreement, Keros received $200 million upfront, is eligible to receive potential development, registration, and commercial milestones north of $1.1 billion, as well as tiered royalties. Keros collected a $10 million development milestone when the first patient in Takeda’s Phase 3 trial (RENEW) for MDS was dosed in July 2025. Elritercept is also undergoing assessment as a monotherapy and in combination with Incyte’s (INCY) Janus kinase inhibitor Jakafi (ruxolitinib) in a Phase 2 trial (RESTORE) for the treatment of MF-associated cytopenias.

Looking at the larger and more advanced indication, MDS is a group of bone marrow disorders where patients experience severe fatigue, anemia, bleeding, iron overload, cardiovascular disease, and potential progression to acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with a median survival range of one year (for high-risk MDS) to nine years (low-risk). The domestic prevalence of MDS is 60,000 to 170,000 with an annual incidence of 15,000 to 20,000.

Treatment options include red blood cell transfusions, which provide relief from anemia, but increase the risk of cardiovascular disease and AML; erythroid stimulating agents (ESAs), which promote red blood cell production but have limited impact on patients with (red blood cell hormone) erythropoietin levels above 200 (normal is 4 to 26); Bristol-Myers Squibb’s (BMY) and Merck’s (MRK) anti-ActRIIB ligand trap Reblozyl (luspatercept), which is approved in both first and second-line settings for lower-risk MDS but has only achieved limited success in high transfusion burden [HTB] patients; and Geron’s (GERN) oligonucleotide telomerase inhibitor Rytelo (imetelstat), which was approved in a second-line setting (after failing on ESAs) for lower-risk MDS with HTB anemia in June 2024. That said, Bristol Myers believes peak sales for Reblozyl can reach $4 billion.

For this indication, elritercept underwent Phase 2 evaluation in patients with very low-, low-, or intermediate-risk MDS and results were encouraging. In 81 evaluable patients, the therapy achieved a 56% overall erythroid response, defined as achieving one of three parameters, one of which is transfusion independence (TI) for at least eight weeks (at 24 weeks) in transfusion dependent patients who required > 2 red blood cell units transfused at baseline. In the HTB group (n=46), elritercept achieved a 50% response rate. The strength of these data advanced elritercept to the pivotal RENEW study for low-risk MDS with a primary endpoint of TI for eight weeks within 24 weeks of treatment. It also provided the marketability for the out-licensing deal.

Recent Intrigue

News of the Takeda partnership sent shares of KROS on a four-day 23% rally, culminating with a $70.00 close on December 6, 2024, representing its second-highest session-end price of the year. Six days later, they plummeted 73% to $18.43 after the company announced that it had suspended 3.0 mg/kg and 4.5 mg/kg dosing of its modified ActRIIB ligand trap cibotercept in a Phase 2 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) trial due to pericardial effusion (excess fluid buildup in the sac-like structure (pericardium) surrounding the heart) in an unspecified number of patients.

With the stock trading at a discount to cash, activist investor ADAR1 Capital Management amassed a then 11% stake that prompted a strategic review and the adoption of a shareholder rights plan to fend off takeover overtures in April 2025. The PAH trial and indication were scrapped in May, triggering a corporate restructuring that reduced Keros’ workforce by 45% to ~85 employees at a cost of $3.6 million. The company’s strategic review was completed on August 6, 2025, with management electing to completely abandon cibotercept to focus solely on KER-065.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Sitting on cash and equivalents of $690 million at the end of Q2 and only one program to fund, Keros announced that it planned to return $375 million to stockholders with details to be announced at a future date. After giving effect to that reduction, the company’s operating runway extends into 1H28.

A once unanimously bullish sell-side analyst community is now mixed on Keros’ prospects, as can be seen below.

The Street does not appear to be assigning any value to KER-065, with all revenue this decade coming from milestone or royalty payments from Takeda.

Verdict:

The company’s future boils down to the results of two Takeda clinical trials and the success of its KER-065 program, which has not yet been tested on DMD patients and won’t enter Phase 2 evaluation until 1Q26 at the earliest.

That said, with its slow cash burn, potential for KER-065 in other indications – due to its muscle building and fat reduction profile – while trading at a small discount to cash, Keros merits a very small dart throw but only for aggressive investors.

data.title: XSMO: The Appeal Of Momentum To Invest In Small-Caps
data.description:
Investing in small-cap companies can be a formidable source of performance, but it traditionally comes with higher risks: it is essential to optimize your risk-adjusted returns above everything else.

The traditional way to access U.S. small-cap stocks is with the S&P 600. Today, I will look at the Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:XSMO), applying a momentum strategy to the S&P 600. I think that this fund is a buy opportunity, as the momentum strategy is applied rigorously and results in a stable fund, more than the S&P 600, that we will look into as a reference with the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) to assess if XSMO’s strategy makes sense. The short answer is yes, it does.

Why investing in Small-caps is something you should consider

I previously covered the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:VB) here, and gave a few arguments in favor of a small-cap investment. The main arguments are:

  • Historically, the outperformance relationship between small and large-caps is working in cycles. Currently, we are on the longest large-cap outperformance cycle ever (14 years, the average is 11 years).

  • Small-cap companies are currently trading at all time low valuations with regard to the S&P 500.

  • M&A transactions involving small-caps are surging in volume, and on average they are executed with a 30% premium. M&A activity is projected to increase further.

Performance and Momentum in Small-Caps

The case for momentum strategies is usually strong in terms of pure performance, but the main issue with them is that they often come with a high volatility and portfolio turnover. For XSMO, the high portfolio turnover is here (consistent with the strategy), but the volatility is surprising. XSMO’s standard deviation is closely aligned with that of IJR, and even slightly lower on longer time horizons: 22.26% (3 years), 21.34% (5 years), and 20.61% (10 years) against 22.13% (3 years), 21.60% (5 years), and 20.86% (10 years) for IJR. Same thing goes for the last 5 years maximum drawdown: -22.65% vs. -23.22%. The strategy of XSMO appears to translate to a higher flexibility making the fund less volatile, and this is often the attention point with momentum strategies.

If we look at Sharpe ratios, to see how the risk is remunerated, this is also a very strong case for XSMO: 0.56 (3 years), 0.55 (5 years), and 0.56 (10 years) against 0.26 (3 years), 0.47 (5 years), and 0.44 (10 years) for IJR. Two takeaways from this: the risk adjusted returns of XSMO are extremely stable, and the fund is able to outperform a market that is not very dynamic thanks to its strategy. Lower risks, higher returns.

Let’s look at the total return:

XSMO is consistently delivering a superior performance, crushing the market on 1Y and 3Y time horizons. The fund’s advantage is clear when small-caps are showing poor performances due to abrupt rotations and economic recoveries: XSMO’s strategy appears to deliver returns in all market conditions.

Concentration, Turnover, and Sector Allocation

Naturally, the strategy of the fund results in a focused portfolio, evident when comparing it to the 637 companies inside of IJR’s portfolio. XSMO is holding 117 companies with a 26.94% top 10 concentration, against 6.72% for IJR. The high concentration of the fund is deliberate in the momentum strategy, chasing the strongest price performers. This chase results in a high 115% portfolio turnover, meaning that you should not spend too much time looking at the fund’s holdings, the portfolio will be entirely different every year.

To construct its portfolio, XSMO is tracking the S&P Smallcap 600 Momentum Index with a quarterly rebalancement. The fund uses a rule-based momentum strategy with quality filters applied to assess systematic price strength. The investment universe is the same as the one of IJR, but the approach is so different from the market cap weighting of IJR that the two portfolios are very different. The goal of XSMO is to adapt quickly to market shifts and deliver alpha in all market conditions, while IJR is simply replicating the market.

The high concentration level of XSMO is also obvious when looking at the sectoral split:
